1: Chapter 30 opens the Book of Consolation (chapters 30–33) — the concentrated section of restoration oracles that stands as the theological counterweight to all the judgment prophecies preceding them. The word that came to Jeremiah from YHWH: Thus says YHWH, the God of Israel: Write in a book all the words that I have spoken to you. The command to write down the restoration oracles is unusual in the prophetic literature — most prophetic oracles were delivered orally. The writing command implies that these words are especially important for preservation and later consultation.
2: For behold, days are coming, declares YHWH, when I will restore the fortunes of my people, Israel and Judah, declares YHWH, and I will bring them back to the land that I gave to their fathers, and they shall take possession of it. The restoration promise encompasses both kingdoms — Israel and Judah. The division of the kingdom (931 BC) and the Assyrian destruction of the north (722 BC) left the 10 northern tribes in perpetual exile. Jeremiah's restoration vision is not merely of Judah from Babylon but of the full reunification of the covenant people. The New Testament reads this as fulfilled in Christ's gathering of the scattered children of God from all nations (Ephesians 2:11–22).
3: The restoration promise is grounded in the original patriarchal land-grant: the land that I gave to their fathers. The gift is prior to the exile, prior to the covenant violation, prior to the judgment — YHWH's original intent for the covenant people was land-possession and flourishing. The exile is an interruption, not a cancellation, of the original divine purpose.
4: These are the words that YHWH spoke concerning Israel and Judah. The double-address (Israel and Judah) confirms that the restoration vision is comprehensive. The oracles in chapters 30–31 alternate between specific addresses to Ephraim (the north) and Judah (the south), reflecting the comprehensive scope of YHWH's restoration intention.
5: Thus says YHWH: We have heard a cry of panic, of terror, and no peace. The opening of the restoration vision paradoxically begins with judgment imagery: a cry of panic, terror, and no peace. The no peace (ein shalom) inverts the false-prophet message of peace — before there is genuine shalom, there must be the honest acknowledgment of the present shalom-lessness. The Book of Consolation begins with a realistic description of the crisis before offering the redemption.
6: Ask now, and see, can a man bear a child? Why then do I see every man with his hands on his stomach like a woman in labor? Why has every face turned pale? The labor-pain image here applied to men is a grotesque picture of panic — men clutching their stomachs in the posture of a woman in labor, their faces pale with terror. In a culture where male composure in battle was a mark of honor, this image of men reduced to labor-pains posture captures the complete psychological collapse that accompanies the Babylonian invasion and siege.
7: Alas! That day is so great there is none like it; it is a time of distress for Jacob; yet he shall be saved out of it. Jacob's trouble (tzarat ya'aqov) — one of the most debated phrases in prophetic eschatology. The day of Jacob's trouble is incomparably great — no historical event like it. Yet the sentence contains the hinge word yet (uminenah): out of this incomparable distress, Jacob shall be saved. The darkness is real and comprehensive; the salvation is equally real and comprehensive. This verse becomes a touchstone for later Jewish and Christian eschatology regarding a period of unprecedented tribulation preceding final redemption (cf. Daniel 12:1; Matthew 24:21).
8: And it shall come to pass in that day, declares YHWH of hosts, that I will break his yoke from off your neck, and I will burst your bonds, and foreigners shall no more make a servant of him. The yoke-breaking imagery from chapter 27–28 returns — but now YHWH himself breaks the yoke, and it stays broken. The wooden yoke Hananiah broke was replaced by iron (Jeremiah 28:13); the iron yoke YHWH will break will not be replaced. The freedom from foreign servitude is permanent. The foreigners shall no more make a servant is the definitive end of exile and subjugation.
9: But they shall serve YHWH their God and David their king, whom I will raise up for them. The restored community will serve YHWH (not foreign powers) and David their king (et-YHWH Eloheihem ve'et David malcham). The reference to David after David's death (c. 971 BC) cannot be the historical David but the Davidic Messiah — the coming king in David's line who will reign over the reunited people (cf. Hosea 3:5; Ezekiel 34:23–24; Jeremiah 37:24–25). The NT identifies this David-king as Jesus, the son of David (Matthew 1:1; Luke 1:32–33; Revelation 22:16).
10: Then fear not, O Jacob my servant, declares YHWH, nor be dismayed, O Israel; for behold, I will save you from far away, and your offspring from the land of their captivity. Jacob shall return and have quiet and ease, and none shall make him afraid. The covenant reassurance formula — fear not (al-tira) — is one of the most common divine addresses in the prophets (cf. Isaiah 41:10, Jeremiah 43:1; Jeremiah 46:27–28). Quiet and ease (shaqat vasha'anan): the shalom of settled, untroubled life — the opposite of the panic and labor-pains of vv5–7. The transformation from Jacob's trouble to Jacob's quiet-ease is YHWH's act of redemption.
11: For I am with you to save you, declares YHWH; I will make a full end of all the nations among whom I scattered you, but of you I will not make a full end. I will discipline you in just measure, and I will by no means leave you unpunished. The covenant distinction: YHWH will make a full end (kalah) of the nations that served as his instruments of judgment, but not of Israel. The discipline (yisartika) is just and measured — not the full covenant destruction. The covenant relationship is the reason Israel survives while the nations are consumed: Israel belongs to YHWH in a way the instrument-nations do not.
12: For thus says YHWH: Your hurt is incurable, and your wound is grievous. The restoration vision pauses to acknowledge the full severity of the present situation before the healing oracle. Incurable hurt (shever enoush) and grievous wound (machalaych) — terms of terminal illness applied to the national situation. The healing that follows will be all the more remarkable because it is healing of what is, from a human standpoint, beyond healing.
13: There is none to uphold your cause, no medicine for your wound, no healing for you. The political situation: no ally, no mediator, no therapy that can address the problem. The abandonment of the divine-medicine-for-your-wound framing anticipates the healing oracle that follows — YHWH himself will do what no human physician or political ally can do.
14: All your lovers have forgotten you; they do not seek you; for I have dealt you the blow of an enemy, the punishment of a merciless foe, because your guilt is great, because your sins are flagrant. The lovers (political allies, cf. Jeremiah 22:20) have abandoned the wounded Israel. The wound Israel suffers is not from a hostile foreign enemy in the ordinary sense but from YHWH himself — the divine lover has struck the unfaithful partner. The blow of an enemy from YHWH's own hand is the shock that drives the healing oracle: the one who wounded can also heal.
15: Why do you cry out over your hurt? Your pain is incurable. Because your guilt is great, because your sins are flagrant, I have done these things to you. The rhetorical question challenges the self-pity that focuses only on the suffering without acknowledging its covenantal source. The wound is self-inflicted in the covenant framework: Israel's great guilt and flagrant sins are the cause of YHWH's blow. Acknowledging this is necessary before healing can occur.
16: Therefore all who devour you shall be devoured, and all your foes, every one of them, shall go into captivity; those who plunder you shall be plundered, and all who prey on you I will make a prey. The reversal oracle begins: the devourer will be devoured, the plunderer plundered. This is the lex talionis applied to nations: the judgment they meted out on Israel will be applied to them. The nations that served as YHWH's instruments will not escape accountability for their cruelty (cf. Isaiah 10:5–19 — YHWH uses Assyria, then judges Assyria for pride and excess; Habakkuk 2:6–20).
17: For I will restore health to you, and your wounds I will heal, declares YHWH, because they have called you an outcast: It is Zion, for whom no one cares! The healing oracle arrives: I will restore health to you (arukah e'aleh lach). The incurable wound (v12) that no physician or ally could treat, YHWH will heal. The motivation for the healing is partly YHWH's response to the nations' contemptuous dismissal: they called Israel an outcast (niddach) — cast out, rejected, valueless. YHWH's restoration of Israel is a vindication of his own covenant commitment against those who wrote Israel off as permanently abandoned.
18: Thus says YHWH: Behold, I will restore the fortunes of the tents of Jacob and have compassion on his dwellings; the city shall be rebuilt on its mound, and the palace shall stand where it used to be. The physical restoration: the tent-settlements (the broader dwelling-places of the people) and the city of Jerusalem will be rebuilt. On its mound (al-tilibah): the city will rise from its ruins on the same site, not relocated. This is a claim about physical continuity — the same city, the same location, rebuilt by the same God who allowed its destruction.
19: Out of them shall come songs of thanksgiving, and the voices of those who celebrate. I will multiply them, and they shall not be few; I will make them honored, and they shall not be small. The restoration community will be characterized by thanksgiving and celebration — the sounds of joy that the judgment section silenced (Jeremiah 7:34; Jeremiah 16:9; Jeremiah 25:10). Multiply, not few; honored, not small: the reversal of exile's demographic and social diminishment. The restored community will grow and gain honor, the opposite of the shameful scattering.
20: Their children shall be as they were of old, and their congregation shall be established before me, and I will punish all who oppress them. The children of the restored community will be like the children of the covenant's best days — the ideal of each generation reflecting the covenant faithfulness of their ancestors. The congregation established before me (edatam lefanai takun): the covenant assembly reconstituted and presented before YHWH. The protection of the restored community is guaranteed: YHWH will punish their oppressors.
21: Their prince shall be one of themselves; their ruler shall come out from their midst; I will make him draw near, and he shall approach me, for who would dare of himself to approach me? declares YHWH. The restored community's leader will be a native son — not a foreign overlord but one from within the covenant people. The leader's access to YHWH (draw near, approach me) is the language of priestly mediation — this figure combines royal and priestly functions, approaching YHWH in the way the high priest approached the holy of holies. The question who would dare of himself to approach me? signals the uniqueness and sacredness of this access: only YHWH can authorize such direct access. The NT reads this through the lens of Christ as the one mediator who has opened access to the Father (Hebrews 10:19–22; 1 Timothy 2:5).
22: And you shall be my people, and I will be your God. The covenant formula in its simplest and most ancient form (cf. Genesis 17:7–8; Exodus 6:7; Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 24:7; Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:28; Revelation 21:3). The entire complex of judgment, exile, and restoration in chapters 1–30 is ultimately in service of this simple covenant statement. The purpose of everything YHWH has done — the plucking up and tearing down, the building and planting — is the restoration of this fundamental covenant relationship.
23: Behold the storm of YHWH! Wrath has gone forth, a whirling tempest; it will burst upon the head of the wicked. A brief return to judgment imagery (cf. Jeremiah 23:19 — the same verse nearly verbatim) within the restoration section. The storm of YHWH's wrath still falls on the wicked — the restoration of Israel does not mean the abolition of divine justice. The covenant community's restoration is not at the expense of righteousness but through and after the full execution of divine justice.
24: The fierce anger of YHWH will not turn back until he has executed and accomplished the intents of his heart. In the latter days you will understand it. The closing verse of chapter 30 (nearly identical to Jeremiah 23:20) anticipates the latter days (be'acharit hayamim) as the time when full understanding of YHWH's purposes will come. The restoration oracles of chapters 30–33 are not fully transparent in their original historical setting — their full meaning will be understood retrospectively, from the vantage point of their fulfillment. This eschatological reservation applies both to Israel's post-exilic restoration and, in Christian reading, to the fuller fulfillment in Christ and the new covenant.
